title: Caring for broad-edge calligraphy nibs
subtitle: Cleaning, sharpening, and storing dip-pen nibs
excerpt: A broad-edge calligraphy nib lasts for years with basic care. The most common failures are avoidable: dried ink in the slit, a corrosion patch on one tine, or a slight curl at the tip from pressing too hard. This tutorial covers cleaning, light sharpening, and proper storage for Mitchell Roundhand and Brause nibs.
sourceNotes: Nib care practice is consistent with recommendations in calligraphy instruction from the early 20th century onward, including care notes in calligraphic instruction booklets published by nib manufacturers such as William Mitchell in the pre-1928 period.

glossaryTerms.definition: The narrow central channel in a broad-edge nib that carries ink from the reservoir to the writing edge by capillary action.
One of the two prongs of a split nib, either the left or right half of the writing edge.

content.text: A broad-edge nib fails in three ways: the
blocks with dried ink so flow stops; the
corrode so the writing edge is uneven; or the tip burrs from pressing too hard against the paper. All three are preventable or easy to fix with simple tools.

items.name: Soft toothbrush (dedicated to nibs)
items.notes: For scrubbing dried ink from around the slit and reservoir after each session.

items.name: Warm water in a small dish
items.notes: Do not use hot water. Soak the nib for 60 seconds to soften dried ink before brushing.

items.name: Dry cloth or kitchen paper
items.notes: For drying nibs immediately after rinsing. Leaving nibs wet causes corrosion.

items.name: Sharpening stone, 1000-grit / 2000-grit (optional)
items.notes: For re-levelling a writing edge that has become uneven. A piece of wet-and-dry sandpaper also works.

content.text: After each session: rinse and dry

Remove the nib from the holder before the ink dries. Dip it in a small dish of warm water and hold it in the water for 60 seconds. Brush the slit and both tine faces gently with the soft toothbrush. Rinse under running water. Dry immediately with a cloth, patting the nib dry rather than wiping. Leave it flat or standing upright (tip up) in a small cup to air for 10 minutes before storing. A nib that goes into storage damp corrodes within days.

If ink flow stops during a session, the slit has dried. Dip the nib in water and hold it there for two minutes, then press the nib gently on a clean cloth to draw out the loosened ink. If the slit remains blocked after soaking, insert a thin strip of cartridge paper into the slit from the back and draw it through slowly; this clears the blockage without bending the tines.

A broad-edge nib that writes scratchy or skips across the paper has a burred tip. Lay the nib face-down on a fine sharpening stone (1000-grit or finer). Make three or four light strokes forward, pressing just enough to feel the stone. Lift and test on paper. Repeat until the nib writes smoothly. Do not sharpen the underside (reservoir face) of the nib: only the writing face needs attention.

Store clean, dry nibs in a small tin or compartmented box. Nibs touching each other in a jumble chip and scratch the writing edges. A strip of corrugated card in the tin provides slots that hold each nib separately. Label the container: a Mitchell 3 and a Mitchell 6 look identical at a glance.

items.symptom: The nib writes on one side only
items.cause: One tine has corroded or bent lower than the other.
items.fix: Hold the nib under a bright light and look at the writing edge straight on. If one tine is lower, press the higher one gently with a fingernail until both tines are level.

items.symptom: Ink floods out in a thick blob rather than flowing evenly
items.cause: The reservoir is bent too close to the nib, supplying ink too fast.
items.fix: Gently pull the reservoir slightly away from the nib with a fingernail. The reservoir should sit just touching the underside of the nib, not pressing against it.
